debate.title: Switch

defs.def: Point mass - An idealized object with mass but no spatial extent, used to simplify analysis by ignoring rotation and internal structure.
Rigid body - An idealized solid object in which the distance between every pair of points remains constant, so it can rotate as well as translate.
Tangent - A straight line that touches a curve at exactly one point and points in the direction the curve is heading at that point.
Radially - In the direction pointing toward or away from the center of a circular path.
Tangentially - In the direction along the instantaneous velocity of an object moving on a curved path, i.e. perpendicular to the radial direction.
Normal - Perpendicular to a surface or line at a given point.
Constraint force - A force exerted by a surface, track, or other boundary that restricts an object's motion to a particular path.
Kinetic energy (KE) - The energy an object possesses due to its motion, equal to ½mv² for an object of mass m moving at speed v.

debate.question:
A train car is freely coasting around a large circular track. It is then switched to a smaller circular track. How does its speed change? Assume no friction.
The speed stays the same when we model the car as a point mass. It decreases slightly when modeled as a rigid body.
A ball on a string spins faster when its radius is reduced, and the same principle applies as the car transitions to a tighter radius. The string does work because, as the ball spirals inward, its velocity has a radial component aligned with the tension.
The fixed frictionless track exerts only a normal constraint force perpendicular to the car's velocity, so unlike the string it does no work. Curvature changes continuously along the connecting arc, causing the direction of the constraint force to rotate.
This rotation means the force cannot remain perfectly perpendicular to velocity. The car's velocity is tangent to the track at every point. The constraint force is normal to the track, hence perpendicular to velocity.
At every instant, the force and velocity rotate together. Perpendicularity is maintained even as curvature changes. On a changing-curvature path, the constraint force must adjust in both magnitude and direction. The centripetal acceleration behaves differently on the connecting track than it does on either circular section.
Therefore, you should reject . The force adjusts direction to maintain perpendicularity; it rotates to stay normal as velocity rotates to stay tangent. Centripetal force remains perpendicular to the velocity throughout, regardless of how its magnitude varies. Therefore, you should reject [None].
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
The connecting track provides a radially inward force that accelerates the car tangentially. Therefore, you should reject .
The speed increases if we model the car as a point mass. It also increases if we model it as a rigid body.
On a frictionless track, the rails' constraint force does no work, so kinetic energy (KE) is conserved and speed cannot increase. If the rails truly do no work, a point-mass car will not lose speed on the smaller track.
Yet if kinetic energy is conserved while the rails do no work, a rigid body on a frictionless track does not slow down, contradicting . A rigid body has translational and rotational kinetic energy; a point mass has only translational.
On a tighter track, rotational kinetic energy increases at the expense of translational kinetic energy.
This holds only when the body is mechanically constrained to yaw with the track curvature (rigid link between axles and body), which has not been established. Both axles are constrained to the rails, so the body's orientation is pinned by the chord between the two axle positions. On a curved section, this geometry causes the body to yaw as it advances.
Therefore, you should reject . A car whose axles are rigidly fixed parallel to its body cannot roll without slipping on a curved track. The curvature differs at the front and rear contact points on the connecting track, with consequences for yaw and speed. Therefore, you should reject [None].
Without that constraint, total KE stays translational and the speed does not fall. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
There is simply nothing on a fixed frictionless track that can add energy to the car. Therefore, you should reject .
